The Complete Historical Lineage of Naqvi Bukhari Sayyid Families of Hyderabad and Karachi: From Arabia to India to Pakistan

The Naqvi Bukhari Sayyid families of Hyderabad (Sindh) and Karachi represent one of the most historically layered, spiritually rooted, and genealogically preserved lineages in South Asian Islamic history. These families trace their ancestry directly to Imam Ali al-Naqi (AS), through a continuous chain of Sayyids that extends from the Arabian Peninsula into Central Asia, then into the Indian subcontinent, and eventually into modern Pakistan.

Arab Origins and Early Lineage

The foundational lineage begins from:

Imam Ali (AS) ibn Abu Talib → Imam Hussain (AS) → Imam Zain ul Abideen (AS) → Imam Muhammad al-Baqir (AS) → Imam Ja‘far al-Sadiq (AS) → Imam Musa al-Kazim (AS) → Imam Ali al-Ridha (AS) → Imam Muhammad al-Taqi (AS) → Imam Ali al-Naqi (AS)

From Imam Ali al-Naqi (AS), the lineage continues through:

Syed Ja‘far Sani → Syed Ali Asghar → Syed Abdullah → Syed Ahmad Shah → Syed Mahmood → Syed Muhammad Bukhari → Syed Ja‘far → Syed Ali al-Muayyad → Syed Qutb Kamal → Syed Jalaluddin Surkh Posh Bukhari

Central Asian and Bukhari Phase

Hazrat Syed Jalaluddin Surkh Posh Bukhari (RA), one of the most influential early Sufi Sayyids, migrated from Bukhara to Multan, and then to Uch Sharif, where he established a major spiritual and genealogical center.

From Uch Sharif, generations of Bukhari Naqvi Sayyids spread across:

·         Agra

·         Firozabad

·         Lucknow

·         Jaunpur

·         Saharanpur

These regions became hubs of Islamic scholarship, Ahl al-Bayt devotion, and Sufi activity.

Indian Settlement: Firozabad and Agra

From the Bukhari lineage emerged:

Syed Jalaluddin Hussain (Makhdoom Jahanian Jahan Gasht) → Syed Mahmood Nar Nasiruddin → Syed Ilmuddin Bukhari → Syed Jalaluddin → Syed Aleemuddin → Syed Nizamuddin → Syed Safiuddin → Syed Jalaluddin Haider → Syed Ilmuddin → Syed Zain ul Abideen → Syed Shah Nizam → Syed Yar Shah Haider → Syed Jahangir Shah → Syed Shamsuddin → Syed Sharafuddin → Syed Kabir Shah → Syed Qutb Shah → Syed Haider Shah → Syed Ameer Ali Shah

Syed Ameer Ali Shah   Mughal Era Figure

Syed Ameer Ali Shah served as a royal physician (Hakim) in the Mughal court. His position gave him:

·         access to elite political circles

·         influence in confidential matters

·         proximity to royal authority

At the same time, he was recognized as:

·         a Sayyid of Ahl al-Bayt lineage

·         a spiritual personality

·         a man of religious discipline and simplicity

Next Generations: Syed Shakir Ali → Syed Ashiq Ali → Syed Nafees Ali

The lineage continues:

Syed Shakir Ali Shah ibn Syed Ameer Ali Shah
→ known for religious preaching in Firozabad
→ associated with Chishti and Jalali Sufi traditions

Syed Ashiq Ali ibn Syed Shakir Ali
→ known for piety, simplicity, and devotion to Ahl al-Bayt

Syed Nafees Ali ibn Syed Ashiq Ali
→ martyred along with his wife in sectarian violence
→ leaving behind minor children:

·         Syed Amjad Ali

·         Syed Hafeez Ali

·         Syed Muhabbat Ali

Migration Crisis: Firozabad → Agra

After the martyrdom of Syed Nafees Ali:

·         children were rescued by their aunt (Phuphi Amma)

·         relocated overnight from Firozabad to Agra

·         initially lived in a shrine compound

This marked the beginning of a major displacement phase.

Partition Migration: Agra → Lahore → Hyderabad (Sindh)

During Partition:

·         approximately 1500 Sayyid individuals migrated together

·         route included:

→ Agra → Lahore → Multan → Rawalpindi → Hyderabad (Sindh)

Final settlement:

·         Hyderabad (Pakka Qila, Kacha Qila, Hirabad)

·         Karachi (for some branches)

Syed Hafeez Ali and Syed Muhabbat Ali

Syed Hafeez Ali

·         performed Hajj

·         known for piety

·         descendants include Rizvi Sayyids (Hyderabad)

Syed Muhabbat Ali

·         lived in Pakka Qila

·         served in mosque (Imamat + Adhan)

·         died due to tuberculosis

A major incident:

→ after his hospitalization, his house was robbed
→ loss included:

·         genealogical records

·         manuscripts

·         family archives

Hyderabad (Sindh)   Establishment of Sayyid Identity

These families became known for:

·         strong Ahl al-Bayt devotion

·         Azadari traditions (Muharram, Chehlum)

·         religious discipline + simplicity

·         trades such as:

o    shoe manufacturing

o    small businesses

Major Descendant Lines

Syed Qadeer Ali Naqvi s/o Syed Umeed Ali s/o Syed Muhabbat ali s/o Syed Nafees Ali s/o Syed Ashiq Ali s/o Syed Shakir Ali s/o Syed Ameer Ali s/o Syed Haider Shah s/o Syed Qutb Shah s/o Syed Kabeer Shah s/o Syed Sharfuddin Shah s/o Syed Shamsuddin Shah s/o Syed Jahangir Shah s/o Syed Yar Shah Haider s/o Syed Shah Nizam s/o Syed Zainulabdeen s/o Syed Ilmuddin s/o Syed Jalaludin Haider Surhwardi Lahori r.a s/o Syed Safiuddin Shah Bukhari s/o Syed nizamudin s/o syed ilmuddin bukhari s/o syed jalaluddin bukhari s/o ilmuddin bukhari s/o syed nar Nasiruddin mehmood bukhari s/o hazrat makhdom syed jalaluddin Hussain almaruf makhdom jahaniya jahan gasht r.a s/o syed ahmed Kabeer s/o hazrat sultan syed jalaludin haider surkh posh bukhari r.a s/o syed ali almoeed s/o syed jafar abu turab s/o syed Muhammad abul fatah s/o syed mehmood Asghar bukhari s/o syed abu yusuf ahmed Baghdadi s/o syed Abdullah Idrees Baghdadi s/o syed abul hasan ali Asghar almaruf ali ashqar s/o syed jafar sani s/o hazrat syed imam ali naqi al hadi a.s

 

 

 

1.      Adam (A.S)

2.      Seth (A.S) son of Adam (A.S)

3.      Enosh son of Seth (A.S)

4.      Kenan son of Enosh

5.      Mahalalel son of Kenan

6.      Jared son of Mahalalel

7.      Enoch (Idris A.S) son of Jared

8.      Methuselah son of Enoch (Idris A.S)

9.      Lamech son of Methuselah

10.  Noah (Nuh A.S) son of Lamech

11.  Shem son of Noah (Nuh A.S)

12.  Arphaxad son of Shem

13.  Shelah son of Arphaxad

14.  Eber son of Shelah

15.  Peleg son of Eber

16.  Reu son of Peleg

17.  Serug son of Reu

18.  Nahor son of Serug

19.  Terah (Azar) son of Nahor

20.  Ibrahim (A.S) son of Terah (Azar)

21.  Ismail (A.S) son of Ibrahim (A.S)

22.  Bashirli (title: Bim al-Tab‘) son of Ismail (A.S)

23.  Samir (title: Bashimin, Mu‘azzam fi al-Kull) son of Bashirli

24.  Hamran (title: Ismail Dhu al-Awj) son of Samir

25.  al-Dariya (title: Hijran al-Ta‘an) son of Hamran

26.  Sayd (title: Ismail Dhu al-Tab‘) son of al-Dariya

27.  Aqbar (title: Ibrahim Jabih Ashmal) son of Sayd

28.  ‘Ashi (title: ‘Afi Aqbar ibn al-Jinn) son of Aqbar

29.  Madi (title: al-Zarb Halim al-Nar) son of ‘Ashi

30.  Nakhash (title: ‘Alqam) son of Madi

31.  Jam (title: ‘Alqam) son of Nakhash

32.  Taih (Taab) (title: Sayqan) son of Jam

33.  Yadlaf (Talaf) (title: Ra’ima) son of Taih

34.  Baldas (Muhtal) son of Yadlaf

35.  Hara (al-‘Awwam) son of Baldas

36.  Qedar (Dhu Basharah Jazmi) son of Hara

37.  ‘Azam (Azam) son of Qedar

38.  ‘Awad (title: Shimar Mani) son of ‘Azam

39.  Mazri (Barmuz) son of ‘Awad

40.  Ki (Shaibi) (title: Lajamhar) son of Mazri

41.  Zarh (title: Qamar) son of Ki

42.  Nahat son of Zarh

43.  Maqsari (Maqas) son of Nahat

44.  Maqsari (title: Hasan, Zamal) son of Maqsari

45.  Abham (Ayami) (title: Daws al-Fan, Ahmal al-Falaq) son of Maqsari

46.  Iqtad (title: Liyamah) son of Abham

47.  ‘Aymar (Amir) (title: Nidwan Dhu al-Amad) son of Iqtad

48.  Dishan (title: Azd Ariyah) son of ‘Aymar

49.  ‘Aydhani (title: Mafir) son of Dishan

50.  Argha (Ruma) (title: ‘Adwan) son of ‘Aydhani

51.  Shahan (title: Junud) son of Argha

52.  Sanjun (Tahzan) (title: Tasawwur) son of Shahan

53.  Bashirli (title: Bim al-Tab‘) son of Sanjun

54.  Aal son of Bashirli

55.  Adnan son of Aal

56.  Descendants of Adnan bin Aal bin Bashirli (Adnan Forefather of all Northern Arab tribes known as adnani tribes)

56.1          Ma‘ad

56.2          Akk

57.  Descendants of Ma‘ad bin Adnan bin Aal

57.1          Nizar

57.2          Quda'a

57.3          Qunus

57.4          Iyad

58.  Descendants of Nizar bin Ma‘ad bin Adnan

58.1          Mudar

58.2          Rabi‘a

58.3          Anmar

58.4          Iyad

59.  Descendants of Mudar bin Nizar bin Ma‘ad

59.1          Ilyas

59.2          Aylan (Qays Aylan)

60.  Descendants of Ilyas bin Mudar bin Nizar

60.1          Mudrikah (Amir)

60.2          Tabikha (‘Amr)

60.3          Qam‘ah (Umayr)

61.  Descendants of Mudrika bin Ilyas bin Mudar

61.1          Khuzayma

61.2          Hudhayl

62.  Descendants of Khuzayma bin Mudrika bin Ilyas

62.1          Kinana

62.2          Asad

139                    Descendants of Syed Sajjad Ali Naqvi bin Syed Shabab Ali bin Syed Muhabbat

139.1              Syed Ali Naqvi

 

Linked Families

Naqvi Bukhari (Irfan Naqvi Line)

·         same higher lineage

·         connected through marriage

Rizvi Family

·         strong Twelver Shi‘i identity

·         Noha Khwani tradition

Jafri Family

·         scholarly lineage

·         includes Syed Shahid Hussain Jafri

·         (Also connected in relations with Zaidi Saadat)

Religious Identity

The family historically reflects:

·         Core: Ithna Ashari (Twelver Shi‘i)

·         Later layers: Sufi (Chishti, Qadiri)

·         Temporary outward alignment: Barelvi (post-migration)

Uch Sharif Verification

Genealogy was later verified through:

·         Makhdum of Uch Sharif

·         Ajmer Sharif custodians

·         Giving to the decendents of Syed Qadeer Ali Navi, & Mahir ul Ansab in this lineage Syed Shahzaib Ali Naqvi

confirming:

→ Naqvi Bukhari Sayyid lineage

Conclusion

The Sayyid families of Hyderabad and Karachi are not merely migrant groups   they represent:

·         a continuous Ahl al-Bayt lineage

·         a multi-century migration history

·         a blend of scholarship, spirituality, and survival

From Arabia → Bukhara → Uch Sharif → Firozabad → Agra → Lahore → Hyderabad, this lineage has preserved its identity through:

·         faith

·         memory

·         tradition

 

 

FINAL CONCLUSION 

The Naqvi Bukhari Sadat family history represents a comprehensive and authentic record of Islamic genealogy, Ahl al-Bayt lineage, and South Asian migration history. This study traces the genealogical roots of the Naqvi Bukhari Sadat family from Imam Ali al-Naqi (peace be upon him), establishing its identity within the Naqvi Sayyid tradition and its integration into the Bukhari Sadat lineage through Uch Sharif.

The history of the Naqvi Bukhari Sadat family includes multiple phases of migration, including movement from Central Asia to the Indian subcontinent and later migration to Pakistan during the Partition of 1947. This genealogical continuity is supported by shrine-based verification, oral traditions, preserved records, and cross-family confirmations.

The Naqvi Bukhari Sadat lineage also reflects a unique synthesis of Sufi spirituality and Ahl al-Bayt-centered belief, including affiliations with Chishtiyya, Qadiriyya, and Jalaliyya orders. Cultural practices such as Muharram traditions and Azadari further reinforce the continuity of Sayyid identity across generations.

Despite the loss of original genealogical manuscripts, the preservation and reconstruction of lineage demonstrate the resilience of the Naqvi Bukhari Sadat family. This work contributes significantly to the fields of Islamic genealogy, Sayyid family history, and Ahl al-Bayt heritage studies.

Naqvi Bukhari Sadat Family History, Sayyid Family Genealogy, Ahl al-Bayt Lineage, Naqvi Sayyid History, Bukhari Sadat Lineage, Islamic Genealogy South Asia, Sadat Migration History, Uch Sharif Sayyid Lineage, Sufi and Shia Heritage, Sayyid Nasab Documentation, Naqvi Bukhari Family Tree, Sadat History Pakistan India

This book presents a complete and verified historical and genealogical study of the Naqvi Bukhari Sadat family. It traces lineage from Imam Ali al-Naqi through migration, spiritual traditions, and cultural continuity across Central Asia, India, and Pakistan, highlighting Ahl al-Bayt heritage and Sayyid identity.

Bukhari Sayyids
Sayyid Family History
Islamic Genealogy

Sadat Migration
Sufi Traditions
Shia-Sufi Heritage
Uch Sharif Lineage
Naqvi Bukhari Identity

A complete historical and genealogical record of the Naqvi Bukhari Sadat family, preserving lineage, migration, and Ahl al-Bayt spiritual legacy across generations.
